title: Week 1 Challenge — Analyse Two NZ Companies
objectives: Apply P/E, EPS and dividend yield metrics to compare two real NZ companies
Interpret a chart description and identify trend, support and resistance
Write a structured investment analysis comparing two NZ stocks

vocabulary.definition: Valuing a company by comparing it to similar listed companies on metrics like P/E, EV/EBITDA and dividend yield. The most common valuation method in practice.

questions.question: Infratil has 700 million shares on issue and earned $280 million net profit. Its share price is $11.20. What are its EPS and P/E ratio?
options: EPS $0.25, P/E 44.8× — a high multiple reflecting Infratil's infrastructure asset quality
EPS $0.40, P/E 28× — accurate for Infratil's scale of operations
EPS $4.00, P/E 2.8× — calculated using millions not billions
EPS $0.10, P/E 112× — Infratil is massively overvalued on this analysis
questions.explanation: EPS = $280,000,000 ÷ 700,000,000 = $0.40. P/E = $11.20 ÷ $0.40 = 28×. At 28× P/E, Infratil is above the NZX 50 average (approximately 20–25×), reflecting its high-quality portfolio of infrastructure assets including Vodafone NZ, Wellington Airport, and renewable energy investments.

questions.question: A share is in a confirmed downtrend. Its 50-day moving average crosses BELOW its 200-day moving average. What is this pattern called and what does it signal?
options: A golden cross — a bullish signal that the medium-term trend has turned positive
A death cross — a bearish signal that medium-term momentum has turned negative relative to the long-term trend
A support bounce — the price is likely to recover from this level
An overbought signal — the RSI has risen above 70
questions.explanation: A death cross occurs when the 50-day MA crosses BELOW the 200-day MA — the opposite of a golden cross. It is considered a bearish long-term signal, suggesting medium-term momentum has turned negative. In a confirmed downtrend, a death cross reinforces the bearish outlook. Note: moving averages lag — a death cross often confirms a downtrend that has been underway for weeks.

questions.question: Company X has a P/E of 8× and Company Y has a P/E of 45×. Company X is a coal mining company in structural decline. Company Y is a high-growth SaaS software business growing at 35% per year. Which statement best evaluates this comparison?
options: Company X is clearly better value — 8× P/E is less than half the market average
Company Y is clearly overvalued — no company is worth 45× earnings

Company X's low P/E likely reflects market expectation of declining earnings, while Company Y's high P/E reflects high growth expectations — each valuation may be appropriate for its respective business
Both are mispriced — the market always settles on the fair P/E of approximately 20× for all companies
questions.explanation: P/E is always relative to growth and business quality. A coal miner in structural decline at 8× P/E may not be cheap at all — if earnings fall 20% next year, the effective P/E rises. A SaaS business at 45× with 35% growth has PEG = 45/35 = 1.3 — not outrageous. The market attempts to price these differences. Low P/E on a declining business is often correct, not an opportunity.

questions.question: Which combination of signals gives the STRONGEST buy signal from both fundamental and technical perspectives?
options: High P/E ratio with 200-day MA below 50-day MA
Low P/E relative to peers, earnings growing strongly, share price recently broke above 12-month resistance on high volume
Falling EPS with RSI reading of 28 (oversold) and no dividend
Average P/E for sector, sideways price trend for 6 months, low trading volume
questions.explanation: Option B combines fundamental attractiveness (low P/E relative to peers + strong EPS growth = potentially undervalued quality business) with a strong technical signal (breakout above resistance on high volume = market confirming the bullish view). This combination — cheap on fundamentals AND breaking out technically — is what many experienced investors look for. Options A, C and D each have concerning elements (high P/E, falling EPS, or no directional momentum).

questions.question: What does it mean when a share's price repeatedly bounces off $15.50 over 18 months?
options: $15.50 is the NZX-approved minimum price for this share
$15.50 is a resistance level — sellers consistently dominate at this price
$15.50 is a support level — buyers consistently step in at this price, preventing further falls
$15.50 is the intrinsic value calculated by the NZX each quarter
questions.explanation: A price level where a share repeatedly stops falling and reverses is a support level. Buyers consistently find value at $15.50 and step in — creating demand that absorbs selling pressure. Support levels are identified empirically from price history, not set by any authority. A decisive break below $15.50 on high volume would be a significant bearish signal — former support often becomes resistance once broken.

activities.title: Compare Two NZ Companies — Full Analysis
activities.setup:
You are comparing two NZ companies as potential investments.

Company A: Spark NZ (SPK)
Share price: $4.18
Shares on issue: 1,860 million
Net profit: $408 million
Annual dividend: $0.275/share (fully imputed)
52-week range: $3.85 – $4.72
Chart description: Clear downtrend over past 12 months from $4.72 high. Bounced twice from $3.85. 50-day MA below 200-day MA. RSI at 38 (not yet oversold).

Company B: Fisher and Paykel Healthcare (FPH)
Share price: $21.40
Shares on issue: 579 million
Net profit: $310 million
Annual dividend: $0.38/share (fully imputed)
52-week range: $18.90 – $23.80
Chart description: Recovery uptrend from $18.90 low. 50-day MA recently crossed above 200-day MA (golden cross). RSI at 58 — healthy momentum.
activities.challenge:
For both companies, calculate:
1. EPS
2. P/E ratio
3. Dividend yield
4. Market capitalisation

Then:
5. Compare the P/E ratios — which company is more expensive and what might justify this?
6. Compare dividend yields — which is more attractive for an income investor?
7. Summarise the technical picture for each company in two sentences.
8. Write a four-sentence investment opinion: which company would you prefer and why? Consider both fundamental and technical factors.
hints: EPS = net profit ÷ shares. P/E = price ÷ EPS. Yield = dividend ÷ price × 100. Market cap = price × shares.
When comparing P/E: FPH is a high-growth medical device company; Spark is a mature telco. What does that imply about which deserves a higher multiple?
For the technical summary: look at MA position (golden/death cross), RSI level, trend direction and 52-week position.
activities.solution:
1–4. Calculated metrics:

Spark NZ (SPK):
EPS = $408M ÷ 1,860M = $0.219/share
P/E = $4.18 ÷ $0.219 = 19.1×
Dividend yield = $0.275 ÷ $4.18 × 100 = 6.58%
Market cap = $4.18 × 1,860M = $7.77 billion

Fisher and Paykel Healthcare (FPH):
EPS = $310M ÷ 579M = $0.535/share
P/E = $21.40 ÷ $0.535 = 40.0×
Dividend yield = $0.38 ÷ $21.40 × 100 = 1.78%
Market cap = $21.40 × 579M = $12.39 billion

5. P/E comparison: FPH at 40× is significantly more expensive than Spark at 19.1×. FPH's higher P/E is justified by: (a) high growth expectations — FPH is a global medical device company with significant R&D pipeline and growing international sales; (b) premium quality — FPH dominates its product categories globally; (c) growth investing premium — markets pay up for businesses expected to compound earnings at 15–20%+ per year. Spark's 19.1× is appropriate for a mature telco with 3–4% earnings growth.

6. Dividend yield: Spark at 6.58% is dramatically more attractive for income investors — more than 3.5× FPH's 1.78% yield. A retiree seeking passive income would strongly prefer Spark. A younger investor focused on long-term capital growth would be more attracted to FPH.

7. Technical summaries:
Spark: In a 12-month downtrend. Death cross confirmed (50-day MA below 200-day MA) — bearish medium-term signal. However, bounced twice from $3.85 support and RSI at 38 is approaching oversold territory (below 30 would be oversold). The technical picture is bearish but showing early signs that selling pressure may be exhausting.

FPH: In recovery uptrend from $18.90 low. Golden cross just confirmed — bullish long-term signal. RSI at 58 indicates healthy momentum without being overbought. Current price $21.40 is in mid-range of its 52-week range. Technically stronger picture than Spark.

8. Investment opinion (sample): FPH is the stronger investment for a young long-term investor despite its higher P/E. The company's global market position in humidified respiratory care and surgical products gives it durable pricing power that a telco cannot match, and its earnings growth trajectory (15–20%+ per year) justifies the 40× multiple over time. Technically, FPH's golden cross and healthy RSI confirm the fundamental bullish case. Spark offers a compelling 6.58% dividend yield and is approaching a potentially oversold level, making it interesting for income investors — but the downtrend and death cross suggest waiting for technical confirmation of a reversal before buying.

activities.title: Your Analysis Process
activities.prompt: What did you find harder — the fundamental calculations (EPS, P/E, yield) or the technical interpretation (trend, MA, RSI)? Which felt more useful for understanding whether the company was a good investment? Write three sentences about your analytical process and which approach you found more insightful.
activities.nceaLink: Extra Learning — combining fundamental and technical analysis is the approach used by most professional investors.
